elements.sent-2411-007: It is now widely recognized that gravity exhibits notable similarities to the other three fundamental forces when examined within the framework of conventional gauge theory \cite{Utiyama, Kibble, ish .}
elements.sent-2411-008: In particular, the spin-connection fields that gauge the Lorentz symmetry arise in much the same way as photons and gluons in the Standard Model.
elements.sent-2411-009: It is thus conceivable that these spin-connections could be unified with the Standard Model gauge bosons within an extended non-compact symmetry group, potentially leading to a unification of all known gauge forces.
elements.sent-2411-010: Furthermore, if we accept that gravity itself governs the symmetry structure of this hyperunification then the quest to uncover such a symmetry must naturally lead us to extend the gauge gravity symmetry group $SL(2,C)$ to the broader local symmetry $ SL(2N,C)$, where $N$ defines the degree of the internal $SU(N)$ symmetry as a subgroup.
elements.sent-2411-011: We invoke the $SL(2,C)$ symmetry and its extension, rather than that of the conventional $SO(1,3)$, as the former appears more fundamental in directly capturing the spinorial nature of fermions in spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-012: In the following, we refer to such frameworks as hyperunified theories (HUTs), with particular attention to the $SL(2N,C)$ HUT discussed here in detail.
elements.sent-2411-013: This unification entails the integration of $SL(2,C)$ gauge gravity with $SU(N)$ grand unified theory (GUT).
elements.sent-2411-014: We designate the $SU(N)$ subgroup of the $ SL(2N,C)$ hyperunification as some "hyperflavor" symmetry and the fields associated with its representations as "hyperflavored" fields in contrast to the "neutral"\ spin-connection field of local $SL(2,C)$ symmetry related to the graviton.
elements.sent-2411-015: Intriguingly, the total gauge hypermultiplet of the $SL(2N,C)$ HUT may encompass both spin-1 and spin-2 fields, offering a potential unified framework for describing all fundamental forces -- including gravity -- should some key challenges, typically encountered in such theories, be successfully navigated.
elements.sent-2411-016: In fact, there are many classes of models in the literature where unification of gravity and other interactions goes through a unification of the local Lorentz and internal symmetries in the framework of some non-compact covering symmetry group \cite{cho, hu, per, cham .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Their difficulties are well known and, to varying degrees, they generally appear in the $SL(2N,C)$ HUT as well \cite{jpl .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Firstly, the vector fields in the total gauge hypermultiplet of this group are always accompanied by the axial-vector fields which must be somehow excluded from the theory as there is no direct indication of their existence.
elements.sent-2411-019: Then, while vector fields are proposed to mediate ordinary gauge interactions, the tensor fields from the same hypermultiplet must provide the subtle gravitational interactions required to align with reality.
elements.sent-2411-020: The crucial point lies in the fact that, whereas in pure gravity case, one can solely consider the action being linear in the curvature ($R$) constructed from a tensor field, the unification with other interactions necessitates the inclusion of quadratic curvature ($R^{2}$) terms as well.
elements.sent-2411-021: Consequently, tensor fields in these terms will induce interactions comparable to those of the gauge vector fields in the Standard Model.
elements.sent-2411-022: Moreover, the tensor fields, akin to the vector ones, exhibit now the internal $SU(N)$ symmetry features implying the existence of the multiplet of hyperflavored gravitons rather than a single neutral one.
elements.sent-2411-023: Apart from that, such $R+R^{2}$ Lagrangians for gravity are generally known to contain ghosts and tachyons rendering them essentially unstable.
elements.sent-2411-024: And lastly, but perhaps most importantly, a potential pitfall in hyperunified theories stems from the Coleman-Mandula theorem \cite{18 concerning the impossibility of merging spacetime and internal symmetries.}
elements.sent-2411-025: It is worth noting that this theorem initially surfaced precisely in connection with one of the special cases of $SL(2N,C)$, specifically the $ SL(6,C)$ symmetry \cite{ish1 , used a long time ago as a possible relativistic version of the global $SU(6)$ symmetry model describing the spin-unitary spin symmetry classification of mesons and baryons \cite{feza .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: In contrast, we aim to demonstrate here how, in the $SL(2N,C)$ HUT framework, these difficulties can be naturally overcome in the way as yet unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-027: The key idea is that the interactions of the extended gauge multiplet $I_{\mu }$ in the $SL(2N,C)$ theory -- comprising generally the vector, axial-vector and tensor field submultiplets -- are suitably shaped by the associated tetrad fields.
elements.sent-2411-028: Crucially, these tetrads are assumed to retain their form in the extended theory, continuing to satisfy the invertibility condition as they do in pure $SL(2,C)$ gravity.

elements.sent-2411-041: The paper concludes with a summary in Section 6.
